title: The Palantir Playbook: Why Suing Your Own Customer Was the Only Way Forward

source.original: Tariffs, DOGE & Fixing America: Peter Thiel's Skeptic Solutions

body_html:
When Palantir was founded in 2003-2004, the idea of building a military-focused software startup was considered insane. No venture capitalists wanted to fund it. The company's thesis was straightforward: the intelligence community and military were using low-tech, privacy-violating solutions for terrorism that amounted to security theater — like forcing people to remove their shoes at airports — while lacking the data integration tools that could actually catch threats.

Early visits to CIA headquarters at Langley reinforced Thiel's suspicions that the agencies had fallen far behind Silicon Valley. The eye-scanning security systems on every door were broken and turned off. The gift shop sold merchandise half-made in China. Whether this was a decoy or genuine dysfunction, the signals were unmistakable. A massive technology gap existed, and Palantir believed it could close it in months.

The reality was brutally different. Palantir targeted a billion-dollar military data integration program called DCGS-A, which was a consultant-driven boondoggle that didn't work. What the company expected to replace in six months took ten years. In 2016, Palantir sued the U.S. Army under a 1994 open procurement law, triggering discovery that revealed internal reports showing Palantir's software performed better — along with orders to suppress those reports and emails directing staff to hide the findings.

Every consultant quit when the lawsuit was filed, warning that suing your customer — especially the Army — would result in permanent blacklisting across Washington. Instead, Palantir won at the trial court level, won at the DC Circuit level, forced a reopened procurement process, and finally secured the contract in 2019. The lesson is stark: when the barrier is political rather than technical, only extraordinary persistence and willingness to use legal mechanisms against entrenched interests can break through.
